sources.citation: Mier y Terán, A., Vázquez, I., & Ziccardi, A. (2012). Pobreza urbana, segregación residencial y mejoramiento del espacio público en la Ciudad de México. Sociologias, 14(30), 118–155.
sources.relevance: Discusses the material conditions of housing in Mexican urban contexts, including wall, roof, and floor quality as indicators of poverty and segregation.
sources.key_finding: Physical housing quality, measured through construction materials, is a key dimension of urban poverty and spatial inequality in Mexico.

sources.citation: CONEVAL. (2018). Metodología para la medición multidimensional de la pobreza en México (3a ed.). Consejo Nacional de Evaluación de la Política de Desarrollo Social.
sources.relevance: Defines housing quality and spaces as one of the six social deprivation indicators in Mexico's official multidimensional poverty measurement, operationalized through floor, wall, and roof materials.
sources.key_finding: Deficient construction materials in floors, walls, or roofs constitute a formal social deprivation indicator affecting millions of Mexican households.

sources.citation: Garza, G., & Schteingart, M. (2010). Los grandes problemas de México, Vol. II: Desarrollo urbano y regional. El Colegio de México.
sources.relevance: Provides a comprehensive analysis of housing conditions across Mexican regions, including structural quality indicators.
sources.key_finding: Regional disparities in physical housing quality reflect deep structural inequalities between northern and southern Mexico.

sources.citation: UN-Habitat. (2003). The challenge of slums: Global report on human settlements. United Nations Human Settlements Programme.
sources.relevance: Establishes international benchmarks for adequate housing, including durability of structure (walls, roof, floor) as a core dimension of habitability.
sources.key_finding: Durable construction materials are one of five core indicators used globally to identify slum conditions and inadequate housing.

sources.citation: CONEVAL. (2018). Metodología para la medición multidimensional de la pobreza en México (3a ed.). Consejo Nacional de Evaluación de la Política de Desarrollo Social.
sources.relevance: Access to basic services (water, drainage, electricity) is one of the six social deprivation indicators in Mexico's official poverty measurement framework.
sources.key_finding: Lack of access to basic services in the dwelling is a formally recognized dimension of social deprivation affecting approximately 19% of the Mexican population.

sources.citation: Satterthwaite, D. (2003). The links between poverty and the environment in urban areas of Africa, Asia, and Latin America. The Annals of the American Academy of Political and Social Science, 590(1), 73–92.
sources.relevance: Examines how access to water, sanitation, and drainage in developing countries relates to health, poverty, and environmental sustainability.
sources.key_finding: Inadequate access to water and sanitation services is both a cause and consequence of urban poverty, with disproportionate health impacts on the poorest households.

sources.citation: Sobrino, J. (2014). Housing prices and submarkets in Mexico City: A hedonic assessment. Estudios Económicos, 29(1), 57–84.
sources.relevance: Analyzes how infrastructure service availability (water, electricity, drainage) affects housing values and residential sorting in Mexican cities.
sources.key_finding: Access to piped water and formal drainage significantly increases housing values, reflecting the capitalization of basic services into property markets.

sources.citation: Jiménez, A., & Pérez-Foguet, A. (2010). Challenges for water and sanitation in developing countries: The case of Mexico. Water Policy, 12(2), 270–288.
sources.relevance: Directly examines water and sanitation service coverage in Mexico, including disparities by region and socioeconomic status.
sources.key_finding: Despite significant national progress, access to water and sanitation in Mexico remains highly unequal, with rural and indigenous communities facing the greatest deficits.

sources.citation: Filmer, D., & Pritchett, L. H. (2001). Estimating wealth effects without expenditure data—or tears: An application to educational enrollments in states of India. Demography, 38(1), 115–132.
sources.relevance: Pioneered the use of household asset indices as proxies for wealth in the absence of income/expenditure data, a methodology widely adopted in developing country surveys.
sources.key_finding: A principal-components-based asset index constructed from consumer durables is a robust proxy for long-run household wealth and predicts educational outcomes comparably to expenditure measures.

sources.citation: McKenzie, D. J. (2005). Measuring inequality with asset indicators. Journal of Population Economics, 18(2), 229–260.
sources.relevance: Evaluates the validity of asset-based wealth measures in Mexico specifically, comparing them to consumption-based measures.
sources.key_finding: Asset indices perform well in capturing inequality in Mexico but may underestimate inequality at the tails of the distribution compared to consumption measures.

sources.citation: Vyas, S., & Kumaranayake, L. (2006). Constructing socio-economic status indices: How to use principal components analysis. Health Policy and Planning, 21(6), 459–468.
sources.relevance: Provides methodological guidance on constructing asset-based socioeconomic indices from survey data on household durables and technology.
sources.key_finding: PCA-based asset indices are reliable and valid measures of socioeconomic status when income data are unavailable, though item selection significantly affects results.

sources.citation: INEGI. (2020). Encuesta Nacional de Ingresos y Gastos de los Hogares (ENIGH) 2020: Descripción de la base de datos. Instituto Nacional de Estadística y Geografía.
sources.relevance: The ENIGH includes detailed household asset inventories used as standard measures of material living standards in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Ownership of appliances, vehicles, and ICT equipment varies dramatically across Mexican income deciles, making asset inventories effective stratifiers of socioeconomic position.

sources.citation: Durand-Lasserve, A., & Royston, L. (2002). Holding their ground: Secure land tenure for the urban poor in developing countries. Earthscan Publications.
sources.relevance: Provides a comprehensive framework for understanding tenure security in developing countries, including formal ownership, informal arrangements, and vulnerability to eviction.
sources.key_finding: Secure tenure is a precondition for household investment in housing improvement and a critical determinant of poverty reduction in urban areas of developing countries.

sources.citation: Monkkonen, P. (2011). The housing transition in Mexico: Expanding access to housing finance. Urban Affairs Review, 47(5), 672–695.
sources.relevance: Analyzes Mexico's housing finance system, including INFONAVIT and FOVISSSTE, and how tenure formalization through mortgage access has shaped housing markets.
sources.key_finding: Mexico's expansion of formal housing finance dramatically increased homeownership but created new problems of peripheral, poorly located housing developments.

sources.citation: Salazar Cruz, C. E. (2012). Los ejidatarios en la periferia urbana: Tierra, vivienda y derechos de propiedad. Estudios Demográficos y Urbanos, 27(3), 517–551.
sources.relevance: Examines the intersection of ejidal land tenure and urban housing in Mexico, highlighting how informal tenure arrangements affect housing security.
sources.key_finding: Informal land tenure on former ejidal lands creates persistent insecurity for millions of Mexican households despite decades of regularization programs.

sources.citation: De Soto, H. (2000). The mystery of capital: Why capitalism triumphs in the West and fails everywhere else. Basic Books.
sources.relevance: Influential theoretical framework arguing that formal property rights transform housing from mere shelter into productive capital, directly relevant to tenure formalization debates in Mexico.
sources.key_finding: Formalization of property rights enables the poor to leverage their housing assets for credit and economic advancement, though empirical evidence in Mexico has been mixed.

sources.citation: Ziccardi, A. (2015). Cómo viven los mexicanos: Análisis regional de las condiciones de habitabilidad de la vivienda. Instituto de Investigaciones Jurídicas, UNAM.
sources.relevance: Directly examines Mexican citizens' perceptions of housing conditions and evaluations of housing policy, providing a framework for understanding subjective assessments of habitability.
sources.key_finding: Citizens' evaluations of housing quality and policy effectiveness vary significantly by region and socioeconomic status, revealing a gap between objective conditions and subjective assessments.

sources.citation: Coulomb, R., & Schteingart, M. (2006). Entre el Estado y el mercado: La vivienda en el México de hoy. Universidad Autónoma Metropolitana / Miguel Ángel Porrúa.
sources.relevance: Analyzes the evolution of Mexican housing policy and public perceptions of state versus market roles in housing provision.
sources.key_finding: The shift from state-led to market-oriented housing policy in Mexico generated widespread dissatisfaction, particularly among lower-income households who perceived declining institutional responsiveness.

sources.citation: Moreno, A. (2009). La decisión electoral: Votantes, partidos y democracia en México. Miguel Ángel Porrúa.
sources.relevance: Provides a broader framework for understanding how Mexican citizens form evaluative beliefs about public policy, including the role of institutional trust and political culture.
sources.key_finding: Mexican citizens' policy efficacy beliefs are shaped by partisan identification, media exposure, and direct experience with government programs, with housing being a salient policy domain.

gap_justification: While there is substantial literature on housing policy in Mexico from an institutional or economic perspective, the specific construct of citizens' normative and evaluative beliefs about housing policy efficacy—combining perceptions of housing quality, preferred solutions, and institutional responsibility—is underexplored as a measurable attitudinal construct. Most studies treat housing satisfaction or policy evaluation as dependent variables rather than as a coherent belief system. This construct fills an important gap by capturing the demand side of housing policy: how citizens perceive the adequacy of responses to housing problems and who they hold responsible. In the Mexican context, where housing policy has undergone dramatic shifts from state provision to market-based models, understanding these beliefs is essential for evaluating democratic accountability and policy legitimacy. Furthermore, this construct enables cross-domain analysis linking housing perceptions to broader patterns of institutional trust, corruption perceptions, and political participation.
